# Why Caching Is Harder Than It Looks

Caching starts with a comforting promise: avoid doing the same expensive work twice.

The hard parts appear immediately after that:

- How do we know two requests mean the same thing?
- How long can a value stay fresh?
- Who removes stale values?
- What happens when ten callers miss at the same time?
- What changes when the application runs on more than one node?

HydraCache is designed around this progression. It starts with a local cache, then adds query adapters, then distributed synchronization.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-cache-problem-space
%%| fig-cap: "The main problem areas HydraCache has to address."
flowchart TD
  A[Cache Lookup] --> B[Key Design]
  A --> C[Value Storage]
  A --> D[TTL and Eviction]
  A --> E[Invalidation]
  E --> F[Multi-node Synchronization]
  F --> G[Client and Member Roles]
```

## Source Notes

Use these files when expanding this chapter:

- [Local cache core](../learning/local-cache-core.md)
- [Cache invalidation](../learning/cache-invalidation.md)
- [Unified architecture](../../HYDRACACHE_UNIFIED_ARCHITECTURE.md)
